micro_segments.title: Thinner Gold and Longer Handle to Assist the Kohen Gadol's Arm Movement
The Niashti: A Ring or Leather Sheath for Sound and Heat Protection

Connection Between Red Gold and Kaparah: Linking Incense to Blood Atonement
Rav Ashi's Reduction: Five Types of Gold Rather Than Seven

micro_segments.title: Normally Half a Maneh Morning and Half Afternoon; Today Double for Kodesh Kodashim

macro_segments.title: The Kohen Gadol's Processional Route on Yom Kippur: Middle Path and Retinue

micro_segments.title: Standard Practice: Kohanim Ascend East and Descend West on the Ramp
Yom Kippur Variant (Rashi): Kohen Gadol Ascends and Descends via the Middle

Reasoning: Honor of the Kohen Gadol (Kavodo) and Practical Efficiency

macro_segments.title: Number of Fires on the Outer Altar: The Debate Among the Tannaim

micro_segments.title: Minimum Agreement: Two Fires on Regular Days Plus One Extra for Yom Kippur
Rabbi Yehuda's Position: Four Total (Main, Incense, Remnants, Plus One for Yom Kippur)
Rabbi Yosi's Position: Three Regular (Adding Maintenance Fire) Plus One for Yom Kippur
Rabbi Meir's Position: Four Regular (Adding Overflow Fire) Plus One for Yom Kippur

macro_segments.title: Derivation of the Two Base Fires from the Tzav Passage: Main Fire and Incense Fire

micro_segments.title: First Fire: The Main Pyre for Burning Limbs and Fats (Ma'aracha Gedolah)
Second Fire: Secondary Pyre for Daily Incense on the Gold Altar (Ma'aracha Shniya)
Pesuk Analysis: Multiple Mentions of Ash and Fire Differentiate the Two Fires

macro_segments.title: The Third Fire Debate: Maintaining Fire (Kiyum HaEsh) vs. Lighting Kindling (Atatzot Alista)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yosi's View: A Separate Third Fire Dedicated to Maintaining Constant Flame
Rabbi Yehuda's Counterargument: The Verse Teaches That Kindling Must Be Lit Atop the Altar
Rabbi Shimon's Challenge: Doesn't Obviousness Alone Prevent Non-Kohanim from the Altar?
Rabbi Shimon's Resolution: The Verse Teaches Kindling Must Be Lit on Top, Not That Only Kohanim Can Tend Fire

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir's Fourth Fire: Transferring Unconsumed Remains to a Separate Pyre

micro_segments.title: The Question: What Happens to Limbs Not Fully Consumed Overnight?
Rabbanan's Answer: Return Them to the Main Fire
Rabbi Meir's Interpretation: A Separate Fourth Fire Specified for These Remains
Derivation from Pesuk: Asher Tochal HaEsh Teaches That Only Animal Sacrifices (Not Incense Remnants) Are Returned

macro_segments.title: The Fifth Fire on Yom Kippur: Deriving Its Existence from Redundant Verses

micro_segments.title: Universal Agreement: An Extra Fire Is Added for Yom Kippur Incense

macro_segments.title: Source of Fire for Yom Kippur Incense and the Menorah: Outer Altar or Closest Source

micro_segments.title: The Question: Does Fire for Yom Kippur and Menorah Come from Outer Altar or Gold Altar?
Gezeira Shava on Esh: Linking Fire for Incense, Fire Pan, and Menorah to Outer Altar
Alternative Gezeira Shava: All Three Derive Fire from Closest Available Source
Resolution via Pesuk: Esh Tamid Teaches Menorah Fire Comes from Outer Altar
Final Derivation for Yom Kippur: Linking to Menorah via Gezeira Shava on Esh or Esh Tamid
Confirmation via Pesuk: Velakach Melo HaMachta from Coals Before God Specifies Outer Altar
